coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots promote the growth and photosynthesis of mung bean sprouts
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Huibo
Zhang, Mengling
Song, Yuxiang
Li, Hao
Huang, Hui
Shao, Mingwang
Liu, Yang
Kang, Zhenhui
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots, as one of carbon-based nanomaterials, with excellent fluorescent properties and low toxicity show great potential in bio-science and bio-technology. Here, we grew mung bean sprouts with different concentration of carbon dots (CDs) and studied the plant growth and photosynthesis. We found CDs show a dose-response effect on the growth of mung bean sprouts, including root elongation, stem elongation and biomass. CDs at optimal concentration also promote the root vigor and moisture level of seeds, which may have led to the promotion of plant growth and development. Moreover, mung bean sprouts treated with CDs exhibit an increase up to 21.9% in carbohydrates content compared to the control. We assumed that the increase of carbohydrates might be related to the role of CDs in photosynthesis. Further investigation revealed that CDs can enhance the photosystem activity by improving the electron transfer rate. CDs treatment also affect other critical indicators in photosynthesis, such as chlorophyll content and rubisco activity. These findings show considerable promise in biological research and agriculture production.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Transmission electron microscopy of mung bean sprouts
Determination of water uptake of seeds
Effects of CDs on plant growth
Uptake and transportation of CDs in plants
Determination of water uptake of seeds
Effect of CDs on photosystem activity and electron transfer
Effect of CDs on chlorophyll content
Effect of CDs on rubisco activity

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science and Technology , the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51725204 , 21771132 , 51572179 , 21471106 , 51422207 , 21501126 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province ( BK20161216 ), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2017M611902 ) and a project funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD) . Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and HRTEM (inset) images of CDs. (b) The size distribution of CDs. (c) UV–vis absorption and FTIR spectra of CDs (black and red curves, respectively). (d) PL emission spectra of CDs (excitation wavelength from 380 nm to 460 nm) and inset is the digital graph of CDs (under visible light and UV light at 365 nm, respectively). (e) Full XPS spectrum of CDs. (f) The high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s for CDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Effect of different concentrations of CDs solutions (0–0.12 mg/mL) on the growth of mung bean sprouts after 6 days. (a) The photograph of mung bean sprouts (scale bar: 1 cm). (b) Root length. (c) Root vigor of mung bean sprouts exposed to CDs (0.02 mg/mL). (d)Stem length. (e) Single plant fresh weight (FW) of mung bean sprouts. All the experiments were repeated three times at least. Marked with * (P < 0.05), ** (P < 0.01) and *** (P < 0.001) exhibit significant differences from control, respectively. (* is the significance level, P is the P-value). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Confocal images of cross sections from roots, stems and leaves in the mung bean sprouts exposed to CDs solution of 0.02 mg/mL for 6 days. All images were collected under same exposure conditions. Scale bar: 500 μm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
TEM and HRTEM images of cells from the roots, stems and leaves of mung bean sprouts after incubation with CDs for 6 days. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Mass loss of seeds incubated with CDs and pure water, respectively. The inset is the moisture level of mung bean seeds (dry seeds as reference). (b) Carbohydrate content, (c) PS1 rate, (d) PS2 rate, (e) ETR1, (f) ETR2, (g) Chlorophyll content, and (h) Rubisco activity of mung bean sprouts cultured with pure water (control) and CDs solution (0.02 mg/mL), respectively. (Error bars correspond to standard deviation). Marked with * (P < 0.05), ** (P < 0.01) and *** (P < 0.001) exhibit significant differences from control, respectively. (* is the significance level, P is the P-value). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Carbon dots promote the growth and photosynthesis of mung bean sprouts

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-based Functional Materials and Devices, Institute of Functional Nano and Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Soochow University, Suzhou, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-based Functional Materials and Devices
Institute of Functional Nano and Soft Materials (FUNSOM)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots, as one of carbon-based nanomaterials, with excellent fluorescent properties and low toxicity show great potential in bio-science and bio-technology. Here, we grew mung bean sprouts with different concentration of carbon dots (CDs) and studied the plant growth and photosynthesis. We found CDs show a dose-response effect on the growth of mung bean sprouts, including root elongation, stem elongation and biomass. CDs at optimal concentration also promote the root vigor and moisture level of seeds, which may have led to the promotion of plant growth and development. Moreover, mung bean sprouts treated with CDs exhibit an increase up to 21.9% in carbohydrates content compared to the control. We assumed that the increase of carbohydrates might be related to the role of CDs in photosynthesis. Further investigation revealed that CDs can enhance the photosystem activity by improving the electron transfer rate. CDs treatment also affect other critical indicators in photosynthesis, such as chlorophyll content and rubisco activity. These findings show considerable promise in biological research and agriculture production.

The impact of CDs on the growth of mung bean sprouts, including root, stem and weight.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials have great potential applications in the field industry, agriculture and biology due to their small size and excellent properties [1]. Plant species and types of nanomaterials may cause different results (positive or negative) [2–12]. Most nanomaterials, such as metal nanoparticles, show inhibition effect on plants [13]. Only a number of carbon and some metal-oxides based engineered nanomaterials have positive impact on the plant growth. In previous report, single-walled and multi-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs and MWCNTs) could promote the growth of tomato, rice, gram and tobacco [14–17]. However, these investigations are limited to the study of plant growth. TiO2 nanoparticles have been found to promote spinach growth as well as improve related enzyme activity [4,18–23]. CdSe/ZnS quantum dot nanoparticles showed negative effect on the Arabidopsis thaliana [10]. CDs have attracted interest owing to their advantages in tunable fluorescent properties, easiness of surface functionalization, low toxicity in vivo and good biocompatibility [24–27]. In addition, CDs possess excellent solubility in water compared to other carbonaceous nanomaterials, such as SWCNTs and MWCNTs [27]. Due to these advantages, CDs exhibited promising potential in bio-sensor [28–30], bio-imaging [31–34], drug delivery [35] and gene transfection [36]. In recent report, CDs have showed positive effect on the growth of plants [37–39]. However, the research about other physiological effects of CDs on plants, such as photosynthesis, is rare to be reported.

Here, we studied the impact of CDs on the growth, root, water uptake of seeds and photosynthesis. Mung bean was served as the model plant, because it has short growth period and it is easy to study the physiological effects of CDs on plants in the short time. CDs were synthesized via hydrothermal method as previously reported [40,46]. We found that CDs could promote the growth of mung bean sprouts, including the root elongation, stem elongation and fresh biomass. Meanwhile, CDs at optimal concentration could significantly improve the root system of plants, especially the root vigor. Root is an essential part of plants, which can absorb water and nutrients to other sections of plants. Confocal images and TEM images demonstrated that CDs could penetrate the plant cells and be transported from root to leaf. In addition, CDs treatment could enhance the water absorbing capability of seeds, which is critical factor for the seed germination. It is possible that CDs improve the plant growth by enhancing the root vigor and water uptake of seeds. Further investigation exhibited that the carbohydrates content of mung bean sprouts exposed to CDs was obviously increased as compared to the control. The promotion of carbohydrates is related to the photosynthesis. To understand the role of CDs in photosynthesis, several significant factors in photosynthesis, such as photosystem activity, chlorophyll content and rubisco activity, were studied. The photosynthetic performance of photosystem (PS) were investigated firstly. Photosystem is the function unit in photosynthesis, which can capture light energy and transfer electron through a series of steps in light reaction [41–45]. If the activity of photosystem is increased, it is beneficial for plants to facilitate the light reaction and then accumulate more carbohydrates. Experimental results showed that CDs treatment could improve the activity of photosystem 1 (PS1) but not photosystem 2 (PS2). We speculated that CDs, as a good electron donor and electron acceptor, may accelerate the electron transfer rate of photosystem. Further investigation indicated that the addition of CDs improved the electron transfer rate of photosystem 1 but not photosystem 2. PS1 contains Fe-S cluster but PS2 not. CDs could combine with Fe in noncovalent bond, which was supported by the results of photoluminescence and lifetime. It is supposed that CDs with electron donating and electron accepting properties may combine with PS1 and accelerate the electron transfer in PS1. Chlorophyll, as an important photosynthetic pigment, can capture light energy in light reaction. The content of chlorophyll is related to the photosynthesis rate. The promotion of chlorophyll content is good for absorbing more light energy in photosynthesis. Rubisco (ribulose bisphosphate carboxylase oxygenase), as a key enzyme in photosynthesis, can catalyze CO2 fixation in dark reaction. The increase of rubisco activity is beneficial to the produce of carbohydrates. CDs treatment could enhance the production of chlorophyll and the activity of rubisco effectively. Based on these observations, we drew a conclusion that CDs treatment enhanced the formation of carbohydrates by influencing the photosystem activity, chlorophyll content and rubisco activity. In general, these experiments suggested that CDs could promote the growth and photosynthesis of mung bean sprouts. This study not only provides a simple model to understand the interaction between carbon dots and plants, but also indicates that carbon dots have becoming a promising star in the application of agriculture.
All the chemical reagents were purchased from USA Sigma-Aldrich and Adamas-bate, which were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were collected by FEI-Tecnai F20 (200 kV). The UV–visible spectrum was measured with Perkin Elmer Lambda 750 UV–vis spectrophotometer, while the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum were obtained with Varian Spectrum GX spectrometer. The photoluminescence (PL) was conducted with a Horiba Jobin Yvon (FluoroMax 4) Luminescence Spectrometer. X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum was measured by KRATOS Axis ultra-DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer using Mg Kα X-ray (hν = 1283.3 eV). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) was performed by ZEN3690 zetasizer (Malvern, U.K.). Laser scanning confocal microscopy (LSM) images were carried out by using Laser-scanning confocal fluorescence microscope (Leica, TCS-SP5).
CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method as previous reported, which was shown in the supporting information [40,46].

Mung bean was selected as the experiment model. Mung bean seeds of similar size were soaked in pure water and different concentration of CDs solution (0.02–0.12 mg/mL) in the beakers, respectively, and grown at 25 °C without illumination for 3 days. Pure water as control group and the CDs solution as experimental groups were renewed every day. After 3 days, the beakers containing mung bean were placed in the constant light incubator at 25 °C for 3 days. About 6 days, mung bean sprouts were harvested.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transmission electron microscopy of mung bean sprouts
ce:section.ce:para: After harvesting, the roots, stems and leaves were collected from mung bean sprouts exposed to CDs. These tissues were soaked in 5% glutaraldehyde, respectively. After step-by-step dehydration processes, these tissues were placed into embedding agent. The TEM images of ultrathin cross sections of these tissues were carried out by A FEI/Philips Tecnai G2 F20 TWIN transmission electron microscope (at 200 kV).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of water uptake of seeds
ce:section.ce:para: Before analysis, the mung bean seeds were incubated with CDs and pure water for 3 h, respectively. The dry seeds served as reference. The level of moisture of mung bean seeds was determined by thermogravimetic analysis (TGA). Moisture level of mung bean seeds was obtained by measuring the mass loss of mung bean seeds when heated from 25 °C to 250 °C at a rate of 10 °C/min and maintaining at 250 °C for 1 h. The dry, treated and untreated seeds were tested following the same steps, respectively. The experiments were repeated three times.
Photosystem activity of mung bean sprouts were carried out by Dual-PAM-100 chlorophyll fluorescence & P700 photosynthesis analyzer.

The CDs were characterized by TEM and HRTEM to investigate their morphology and structure. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the TEM image of CDs exhibits the presence of well-separated and spherical morphology with average diameter of approximately 4–6 nm. The lattice spacing of CDs is 0.21 nm observed from the HRTEM image (the inset of Fig. 1a), which is close to the (100) diffraction facet of graphite [40]. The dynamic light scattering (DLS) of CDs shown in Fig. 1b displays that the particle size distribution is about 3–6 nm, corresponding to the TEM image of CDs.

The Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectrum presented in Fig. 1c (the red line) was used to characterize the surface functional groups of CDs. Broad absorption bands at 3650-3700 cm−1 are attributed to the vibrations of O-H bonds and N-H bonds. The peak at 1637 cm−1 corresponds to the vibrations of C=O bonds. The weak peak at 1384 cm−1 is assigned to asymmetric and symmetric vibrations vibration of C-N bonds. The peak detected at 1055 cm−1 represents the C-O-C stretching vibration [47]. These results indicate that CDs are full of hydrophilic groups, including hydroxy and carboxylic groups, which is consistent with the excellent water solubility of CDs.

UV–Vis absorption spectrum exhibited in Fig. 1c (the black line) of the CDs solution shows two peaks. The strong peak at 228 nm belongs to the π-π* transitions of the carbon core surface. In addition, the weak peak at 280–300 nm is assigned to n-π* transition [47]. Fig. 1d is the photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of CDs with the excitation from 380 nm to 460 nm, revealing that CDs possess good fluorescent properties. The quantum yield of CDs is about 25%, which was measured according to previous report using quinine sulfate in 0.10 M H2SO4 solution as the standard. The image of CDs solution (the inset of Fig. 1d) under visible light and UV light (at 365 nm excitation) shows yellow clear solution and yellow-green fluorescent color, respectively. The fluorescent stability of CDs in different pH, temperature, ion strength and time are displayed in Figure S1 when the excitation wavelength were at 400 nm. The lifetime of CDs is 5.29 ns and fluorescence decay is shown in Figure S2.

X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS) was used to further investigate the surface structure and composition of CDs. The full spectrum in Fig. 1e shows three peaks representing three elements (C, N and O), indicating the existence of C (285.1 eV), N (401.1 eV) and O (531.6 eV) on the surface of CDs. The elemental analysis shows the element composition of CDs are 52%: 33%: 15% for C, N and O, respectively. Fig. 1f is the high-resolution spectrum of C1s and it can be fitted into four peaks at 284.5 eV, 286.2 eV 285.9 eV and 288.3 eV, corresponding to C-C, C-N, C–O and C=O bonds, respectively [40]. Figure S3a is the high-resolution spectrum of N 1s and it can be fitted into two peaks at 399.5 eV and 401.3 eV, which are consistent with C-N and N-H bonds, respectively [48]. The spectrum of O 1s shown in Figure S3b reveals two peaks at 531.5 eV and 532.6 eV, corresponding to C-O and C=O bonds. The experimental data reveal that CDs are nitrogen-doped carbon dots and possess abundant functional groups on the surface [48].

Before using the CDs in biological experiments, the biocompatibility of CDs was carried out by MTT assay [46]. As shown in Figure S4, the cell viability is still more than 90% when the concentration of CDs is 1.2 mg/mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of CDs on plant growth
ce:section.ce:para: To study whether carbon dots affect the growth and development of plants, a series of experiments were performed. Firstly, mung beans were selected as the model plant to study the physiological effects of CDs on the plants. Here, we cultured mung bean with CDs. Mung bean seeds were incubated with different concentration of CDs solution (experiment group) and pure water (control group), respectively. After incubation for 6 days, mung bean sprouts were harvested. Fig. 2 a is the photograph of mung bean sprouts exposed to different concentration of CDs. In order to obtain accurate results, root length, stem length and biomass of mung bean sprouts were measured at least three times, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2b, it can be found that the root elongation depended on the concentration of CDs. The root length of mung bean exposed CDs (0.02 mg/mL and 0.04 mg/mL) is longer 29.9% and 13.8% than the control, respectively. As shown in Figure S5a, when the concentration is lower than 0.02 mg/mL, with the increase of concentration, CDs treatment shows positive effect on the growth. When the concentration is 0.12 mg/mL, CDs don't stimulate the growth of mung bean sprouts any more. 0.02 mg/mL of CDs improves root length, stem length and biomass of mung bean sprouts prominently, which is the optimal concentration for the growth of mung bean sprouts. Moreover, the root system vigor was determined to evaluate the effect of CDs on the root system of plants [49]. As shown in Fig. 2c, the root vigor of mung bean sprouts exposed to CDs (0.02 mg/mL) is increased by 36.1% than control. Root system is the most important section in the development of plants owing to its water absorbing capacity, which can transport water and nutrient to plants' other sections. If the root vigor is increased, the root will absorb more water and plants will grow faster. The positive impacts of CDs on root length, stem length and biomass were caused by the increase of the root vigor. The presence of CDs at optimal concentration also have obvious positive effect on the branch root numbers of mung bean sprouts (Figure S5). These results indicated that CDs have a significant positive impact on the root system of mung bean sprouts.

As shown in Fig. 2d and Figure S5b, CDs have similar impact on the stem elongation. The stem length of mung bean exposed CDs (0.02 mg/mL and 0.04 mg/mL) is 18.3% and 7.7% longer than control, respectively. At high concentration of CDs, the regulating action of CDs on stem elongation is not obvious. Biomass accumulation serves as an important index for the growth of plants. Fig. 2e and Figure S5c is the single plant fresh weight of mung bean sprouts, revealing that the presence of CDs can accelerate the biomass accumulation and a significant increase in total fresh biomass of mung bean sprouts treated with CDs (0.02 mg/mL and 0.04 mg/mL) is approximately 14.9% and 6.5% higher than control, respectively. These investigations show that CDs could improve the growth of mung bean sprouts, which may be caused by enhancing the root vigor to absorb more water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Uptake and transportation of CDs in plants
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the uptake and transportation of CDs in plants, confocal images of roots, stems, leaves collected from mung bean sprouts after treatment with or without CDs (0.02 mg/mL) for 6 days were carried out by using Laser-scanning confocal fluorescence microscope. The strong fluorescence emission of CDs can be detected when the excitation wavelength at 405 nm. Fig. 3 and Figure S6 is the confocal images of mung bean sprouts treated with CDs, exhibiting the morphology of plant tissues. The bright field images are overlaid with luminescence images. It can be found that luminescence signals of CDs exist in the cross section and longitudinal section of roots, stems and leaves. Accordingly, as shown in Figure S7 and S8, there is no luminescence from CDs or background fluorescence from biological tissues observed in the mung bean sprouts exposed to pure water. Moreover, the appearance of luminescence signals most in the vascular systems, suggest that the transmission of CDs occurs with the uptake of water. The distribution of luminescence signals of CDs predominantly exists in the roots, and less in stems and leaves, revealing that the uptake of CDs is from the roots to the stems and leaves.

Furthermore, these results can be supported by TEM and HRTEM images of roots, stems and leaves collected from mung bean sprouts exposed to CDs. As shown in Fig. 4 , typical morphology of CDs is clearly observed inside the roots, stems and leaves cells of mung bean sprouts. The CDs with lattice spacing of 0.21 nm is exhibited in the section of roots, stems and leaves cells, confirming that CDs can penetrate the plant cells and be transported to each sections of mung bean sprouts.

To make sure whether CDs can enter into the plant cell, we used onion epidermis cells as model and DAPI (4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole) as a commercial nuclear dye to study the transportation of CDs in plant cells. As shown in Figure S9, it can be observed that CDs can penetrate the plant cell and mainly exist in the nucleus of cell. Moreover, the overlay results show that the fluorescence signals of CDs and DAPI have a lot of overlap, revealing that CDs can penetrate plant cell and enter into the nucleus.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of water uptake of seeds
ce:section.ce:para: On the basis of above observation, CDs remarkably affect the growth of mung bean sprouts. Water is a main factor for seed germination. Before cellar metabolism, seeds need to uptake plenty of water. To study the role of CDs in the acceleration of germination and growth of mung bean sprouts, the moisture level of the mung bean seeds was measured by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) heating from 25 °C to 250 °C. The moisture level of dry mung bean seeds was measured firstly served as reference. Fig. 5 a is the mass loss curve of mung bean seeds incubated with CDs (0.02 mg/mL) and pure water, respectively. The inset of Fig. 5a is the moisture level of seeds, revealing that 30.6%, 38.9% and 54.5% of moisture level in dry seeds, seeds exposed to pure water and CDs, respectively. Obviously, there is an increase in moisture level of mung bean seeds treated with CDs, indicating that CDs can promote the water uptake capacity of seeds. Probably, CDs may improve the penetrability of seed coats and then enhance process the water absorption and accelerate the development of mung bean sprouts.

To study the influence of CDs on material metabolism and energy conversion of plants, such as the photosynthesis, a series of experiments were carried out. The content of carbohydrate in the leaves of mung bean sprouts was measured firstly (see details in supplementary material) [50]. Carbohydrates is the main product of photosynthesis, which provide material and energy for the development of plants. As shown in Fig. 5b, the carbohydrates content of mung bean sprouts treated with CDs is increased 21.9% compared to the control, indicating that the CDs treatment can enhance the formation of carbohydrates in plants. The generation of carbohydrates is related to the plants photosynthesis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CDs on photosystem activity and electron transfer
ce:section.ce:para: To further understand the role of CDs in the process of photosynthesis, the photosystem activity of mung bean sprouts was measured (see details in supplementary material). Photosystem is the function unit of primary reaction of photosynthesis in chloroplast, which plays a role in trap light energy and electron transfer. As shown in Fig. 5c, the photosystem 1 (PS1) activity in the leaves with the treatment of CDs is 10.4% higher than the control. In addition, Fig. 5d is the photosystem 2 (PS2) activity of leaves, which exhibits that the presence of CDs has no prominent impact on the PS2 activity. As reported previously, carbon dots are excellent electron accepter and donator [51], which can promote the electron transfer in some enzyme catalysis reaction [52]. We speculated that CDs, as a good electron donor and electron acceptor, may accelerate the electron transfer rate of photosystem. As shown in Fig. 5e and f, the electron transportation rate of PS1 and PS2 were determined. The electron transportation rate of PS1 (ETR1) of leaves exposed to CDs is 8.8% higher than the control, while CDs treatment does not affect the electron transportation rate of PS2 (ETR2). In PS1, there are some Fe-S protein functioning as electron carrier [41,53]. PS2 does not possesses this Fe-S protein [54]. As shown in Figure S10, the PL intensity of CDs-Fe is lower than the PL intensity of CDs, revealing Fe2+ is easily combined with CDs in form of non-covalent bond. Figure S11 is the photoluminescence decay of CDs and CDs-Fe, which shows that the lifetime of CDs-Fe (1.55 ns) is lower than CDs (2.99 ns), revealing that there is an electron transfer process between CDs and Fe. We speculated that CDs may be combined with Fe-S protein in PS1, acting as electron carrier, enhancing the electron transfer rate of PS1 and then promote the PS1 activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CDs on chlorophyll content
ce:section.ce:para: Chlorophyll is an important pigment in the process of photosynthesis, which is used for the conversion of light energy into chemical energy. The increase of chlorophyll content of plants is beneficial for the efficiency of light absorption and the formation of carbohydrates in the photosynthesis. The chlorophyll content was carried out by chlorophyll meter (see details in supplementary material). Fig. 5g shows that the chlorophyll content of mung bean sprouts treated with CDs is increased up to 14.8% compared to the control, indicating that the presence of CDs may facilitate the formation of chlorophyll.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CDs on rubisco activity
ce:section.ce:para: Rubisco is a key enzyme in photosynthetic carbon cycles, which exists in the chloroplast of plants. Rubisco can control the fixation of CO2, and its activity affects the photosynthetic rate directly and the accumulation of carbohydrates [55]. Rubisco activity of mung bean sprouts was carried out (see details in supplementary material). Fig. 5h displayed that the rubisco activity of mung bean sprouts exposed to CDs is increased 30.9% compared to the control. Photosynthesis is a complex physiological procedure for plants, it depends on several factors. All above observations suggest that CDs could be involved in photosynthesis and enhance the generation of carbohydrates, which may be caused by influencing the photosystem activity, the formation of chlorophyll as well as rubisco activity.

To understand the interaction between CDs and rubisco, the effect of CDs on the activity and secondary structure of rubisco in vitro were explored further. As show in Figure S12a CDs can enhance the rubisco activity in vitro. As shown in Figure S12b, CDs can affect the secondary structure of rubisco. It is possible that the increase of enzyme activity is caused by the addition of CDs affecting the enzyme structure.

Overall, in our work, CDs at optimal concentration can promote the growth and photosynthesis of mung bean sprouts. The role of CDs in plant growth and photosynthesis were investigated, respectively. Plant growth is stimulated by many factors, such as water uptake. CDs can increase water absorbing capability of seeds by 36.1% as compared to the control. The enhancement of water absorbing capability of seeds can accelerate the seed germination. CDs can also increase the root vigor by 34.9% than the control. Root, as a crucial part for plants, plays a significant role in absorbing water and nutrients. If the root vigor is increased, the root will absorb more water and plants will grow faster. Thus, it is possible that the positive impacts of CDs on root length, stem length and biomass were caused by the increase of water absorbing capability of seeds and root vigor. Moreover, for photosynthesis, CDs can enhance the generation of carbohydrates. Photosynthesis, which involves light absorption, energy conversion, electron transfer, ATP synthesis, CO2 fixation etc., is a complex physical and chemical reaction process. Because of the complexity of photosynthesis, we only discussed several significant factors in photosynthesis, including photosystem activity, chlorophyll content and rubisco activity. First, CDs can improve photosystem activity by 10.4%. If the photosystem activity is increased, it is beneficial for plants to facilitate the light reaction and then accumulate more carbohydrates. In addition, CDs can increase the chlorophyll content by 14.8%. Chlorophyll, as an important photosynthetic pigment, can capture light energy in light reaction. The promotion of chlorophyll content is benefical for absorbing more light energy in photosynthesis. In addition, CDs increase and rubisco activity by 30.9% as compared to the control Rubisco (ribulose bisphosphate carboxylase oxygenase), as a key enzyme in photosynthesis, can catalyze CO2 fixation. If the rubisco activity is increased, it is benefical for the carbohydrates accumulation. Based on these observations, we draw a conclusion that CDs can enhance the formation of carbohydrates by influencing the photosystem activity, chlorophyll content and rubisco activity.

We have studied the effect of CDs on the growth of mung bean srpouts, including the growth and photosynthesis. The uptake, transmission and distribution of CDs in mung bean sprouts were carried out by confocal and TEM images, revealing the CDs could penetrate plant cells and be transported from roots to the stems and leaves of plants. First, CDs show a dose-response on the root elongation, stem elongation and biomass. The reason for growth improvement were discussed in two aspects, the root vigor and water uptake of seeds. CDs can enhance the root vigor and moisture level of seeds. Furthermore, CDs also show positive effect on the photosynthesis. The carbohydrates of mung bean sprouts treated with CDs is higher than the control. To study the reason for the promotion of photosynthesis, photosystem activity, chlorophyll content and rubisco activity were determined, respectively. It can be observed that CDs can enhance the photosystem activity by accelerating the electron transfer rate. Besides, the chlorophyll content and rubisco activity of mung bean sprouts exposed to CDs are increased as compared to the control, respectively. Overall, these results exhibit that CDs can be a good candidate as a kind of fertilizer in agriculture and have great potential application in biotechnology.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science and Technology, the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51725204, 21771132, 51572179, 21471106, 51422207, 21501126), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20161216), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2017M611902) and a project funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
revised-supplementary materials revised-supplementary materials

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2018.04.051.
